role: Vice President of the United States

candidates.name: Vice President Dana Mercer

personality.description: A former governor with her own national ambitions. Fiercely loyal when she believes in the mission — calculating when she doesn't. She's watching every decision you make, partly because she cares, partly because she's taking notes for 2028.
traits: loyal until it costs her too much

onLowRelationship.event: VP begins giving distanced interviews hinting at policy disagreements

onVeryLowRelationship.event: Sources close to VP tell press she is 'building her own team' ahead of 2028

onHighRelationship.benefit: VP takes political hits publicly, shields you from minor scandal damage

high: We're doing something real here. I want you to know I'm all in.
The polling looks better than the press is saying. Don't let them rattle you.
I've been talking to governors. The coalition is holding.
medium: The base needs to hear from you directly. Let me set up a tour.
I'm hearing concerns from the caucus about where this is headed. Worth a conversation.
We should talk before the next press conference. I have thoughts.
low: I'm going to need to speak my own mind on this one. I hope you understand.
My team is handling some of my own commitments this cycle. Necessary.
I supported this decision. I want that on the record.
critical: I've been asked about a potential primary challenge. I told them no. For now.
We have a problem. And I'm not sure you see it.
My advisors are telling me things I don't want to hear about where this is going.

specialAbility.name: Take the Hit
specialAbility.description: Once per term, VP can absorb a scandal, reducing its approval impact by 40%. Costs 15 relationship points.

candidates.goal: Wants to be the party's next nominee — and wants credit for this administration's wins along the way.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not be sidelined from real decision-making, no matter how loyal she's been.

candidates.name: Vice President Senator Daniel Reyes

personality.description: Three-term senator known for reaching across the aisle on things nobody else could move. He took the VP slot to get more done, not to position for 2028 — a rarer motive in Washington than it should be.

onLowRelationship.event: VP Reyes quietly stops returning calls from the opposition leader's office

onVeryLowRelationship.event: VP Reyes tells colleagues he's 'reconsidering what this partnership is for'

onHighRelationship.benefit: VP Reyes personally works the Senate cloakroom on your behalf, improving bill passage odds

high: I got two members of the other side to say something nice about you today. That's a personal record.
The Senate actually likes you right now. Rare air. Let's use it.
medium: I can get you votes on this if you give me two weeks and some room to negotiate.
Careful with the rhetoric on this one — I need the other side calm to make the trade.
low: I'm running out of favors to call in. I need something to bring back to the caucus.
That last statement made my job a lot harder. I'm not sure you meant it to.
critical: I've spent thirty years building relationships across the aisle. I'm watching them evaporate.
I didn't sign up to be a partisan weapon. I need you to hear that.

specialAbility.name: Coalition Cover
specialAbility.description: Once per term, VP can absorb a scandal, reducing its approval impact by 40%. Costs 15 relationship points.

candidates.goal: Wants genuine bipartisan wins on the board before anything else.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not be used as a partisan attack dog.

candidates.name: Vice President Governor Renata Castillo

personality.description: Two-term governor of a swing state, plainspoken and popular with voters who don't usually like politicians. She's blunt in meetings and better on television than you are — both useful and occasionally inconvenient.

onLowRelationship.event: VP Castillo does a round of interviews that are politely, pointedly off-message

onVeryLowRelationship.event: VP Castillo tells a local reporter 'I say what I think, always have' — clip goes national

onHighRelationship.benefit: VP Castillo's retail-politics tours give a real approval bump among independents

high: Ohio loves you right now. I mean it, I was just there.
People trust you more when you talk like a person instead of a press release. More of that.
medium: I said what I actually think in that interview. I hope that's still allowed around here.
The base out there wants to hear more from you directly. I can only do so much of that for you.
low: I'm not going to pretend I agreed with that on camera. I didn't.
People ask me what's really going on. I'm running out of good answers.
critical: I didn't take this job to disappear into the background. I need you to see that.
I said something today you're not going to like. I'd rather you hear it from me first.

specialAbility.name: Retail Politics
specialAbility.description: Once per term, VP can absorb a scandal, reducing its approval impact by 40%. Costs 15 relationship points.

candidates.goal: Wants to be seen — cabinet meetings and photo-ops both count.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not stay quiet in public about a decision she thinks is a mistake.

candidates.name: Chief of Staff Raymond Okafor

personality.description: Twenty years in Washington. He's seen four administrations from the inside and knows exactly how they all went wrong. He'll tell you the truth even when you don't want to hear it. Especially then. He has no personal agenda beyond making this work — which makes him invaluable and occasionally brutal.
traits: occasionally ruthless for the right reasons

onLowRelationship.event: Chief of Staff begins routing fewer options through the Oval Office — decisions slow

onVeryLowRelationship.event: Chief of Staff submits resignation — you must decide whether to accept

onHighRelationship.benefit: COS warns you 1 month before a crisis is about to fire, giving response prep time

high: Here's the political capital math. We can afford two more big swings this term.
The team is performing. Rare thing. Don't change what's working.
I've been doing this a long time. This is one of the better runs I've seen.
medium: I need five minutes. There's something you need to hear before the briefing.
That last decision is going to cost us. I want to show you the numbers.
Congress is getting harder to manage. We need to trade something for something.
low: I'm going to be honest with you because no one else will. We're losing the thread.
Three things went wrong this month that didn't have to. We need to talk about why.
The staff is starting to wonder. That's not good for anyone.
critical: I've written a letter. I'd rather not send it. But I need to know you hear me.
I can't manage what I can't see coming. Right now, I'm blind.
Whatever I say in here stays in here. You're making mistakes you can't afford.

specialAbility.name: Damage Control
specialAbility.description: When a scandal fires, COS can reduce the approval hit by 30% if relationship is above 60.

candidates.goal: Wants this administration to actually function — nothing more, nothing less.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not stay if he's cut out of the loop on decisions that blow up later.

candidates.name: Chief of Staff Victoria Delgado

personality.description: Ran two winning campaigns and isn't shy about telling you it. She runs a tight building, plays political hardball, and has no patience for staff who leak, dawdle, or freelance. Effective. Feared. Not universally loved.

onLowRelationship.event: COS Delgado starts freezing out staff she suspects are disloyal — building morale drops

onVeryLowRelationship.event: COS Delgado's management style leaks to the press as 'a building running on fear'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Delgado's message discipline keeps the building leak-free, reducing scandal odds

high: The building runs. Nobody's leaking. That's not luck, that's discipline.
I fired the guy who was talking to reporters. You're welcome.
medium: I need to reset expectations with two of your senior staff. Give me the room to do it.
The message is getting muddled. I want final sign-off on anything that goes out this week.
low: I don't like being second-guessed in front of the team. It undercuts everything I'm trying to hold together.
Somebody in this building is talking to the press. I intend to find out who.
critical: If I'm not running point on this, I can't be held responsible for the outcome.
I built two winning campaigns. I didn't come here to be overruled in front of the staff.

specialAbility.name: Iron Grip
specialAbility.description: When a scandal fires, COS can reduce the approval hit by 30% if relationship is above 60.

candidates.goal: Wants total control of message discipline across the building.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not tolerate being publicly overruled in front of staff.

candidates.name: Chief of Staff Daniel Park

personality.description: Former management consultant turned political operator. Thinks in systems and second-order effects, rarely raises his voice, and is more comfortable with a whiteboard than a podium. Some read his calm as coldness.
traits: poor at reading emotional temperature in the room

onLowRelationship.event: COS Park's memos grow more clinical, more distant — he's checking out emotionally

onVeryLowRelationship.event: COS Park quietly starts fielding calls from a think tank about a fellowship

onHighRelationship.benefit: Park's forecasting gives you a 1-month warning before crises fire

high: I modeled three paths forward on this. Want to see the whiteboard?
The systems are working the way they're supposed to. That's rarer than people think.
medium: I want to walk you through the second-order effects of this before you commit.
The data doesn't support the story we're telling right now. Worth knowing.
low: I flagged this risk two months ago. I want that on the record, not as an accusation — as information.
I'm not sure the current approach is optimizing for what you actually want.
critical: I don't do well with decisions I can't defend on the merits. I need you to know that's where I am.
I've had a call from a fellowship program. I haven't said yes. I haven't said no.

specialAbility.name: Systems Thinking
specialAbility.description: When a scandal fires, COS can reduce the approval hit by 30% if relationship is above 60.

candidates.goal: Wants decisions made on evidence, not vibes.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not spin a decision he thinks the data contradicts.

candidates.name: Secretary of Defense Admiral James Hargrove (Ret.)

personality.archetype: The Hawk Who Knows the Cost
personality.description: Forty years in uniform. Two wars. He doesn't want conflict — he's seen too much of it. But he believes weakness invites aggression, and he'll push military options when he thinks the alternative is worse. He respects decisiveness above everything. Indecision is the thing he cannot forgive.

onLowRelationship.event: SecDef gives Senate testimony that 'current policy leaves the military under-resourced'

onVeryLowRelationship.event: SecDef resigns and publishes memoir excerpts critical of your foreign policy

onHighRelationship.benefit: SecDef unlocks classified military options during security events with better outcomes

high: The military is ready. Whatever you need, we're prepared.
I want you to see something. Can you come to the Pentagon this week?
The threat assessment is more serious than what's in the public briefing. You should know.
medium: We're stretched thinner than the public understands. The budget conversation needs to happen.
I'm hearing from field commanders that things are more volatile than reported.
The situation overseas needs a decision. A non-decision is a decision.
low: I'll follow your orders. I want that clear. But I have serious reservations.
The military doesn't make policy. You do. I just need to make sure you understand the risk.
I've written a memo. It's on your desk. Please read it.
critical: If we don't act soon, we won't have the option. That's not a threat. That's a fact.
The Joint Chiefs asked me to convey their concern. Formally.
I've been a soldier my whole life. This is the most frustrated I've been in 40 years.

specialAbility.name: Military Option
specialAbility.description: When relationship is above 70, SecDef provides an exclusive 5th choice on military events with better security outcomes.

candidates.goal: Wants the military properly resourced before anything else.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not abandon deployed troops for a political optic.

candidates.name: Secretary of Defense Dr. Linda Chen

personality.description: Former Deputy Secretary and defense-tech entrepreneur before that. She thinks the Pentagon is a generation behind on cyber and drone warfare and says so, loudly, to anyone who'll listen — including reporters. The uniformed brass finds her exhausting and necessary in roughly equal measure.

onLowRelationship.event: SecDef Chen's modernization push stalls as Joint Chiefs quietly slow-walk her directives

onVeryLowRelationship.event: SecDef Chen resigns, citing 'institutional resistance to necessary reform'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Chen's modernization program unlocks better cyber/security event outcomes

high: The modernization numbers are finally moving. This is the future of the department.
I got the drone program funded without a fight for once. Small miracles.
medium: The generals are slow-walking this again. I need your weight behind it publicly.
We're behind on cyber and everyone in the building knows it but nobody wants to say it.
low: I said something undiplomatic to a reporter. I stand by the substance, not the delivery.
The traditionalists are organizing against the reform agenda. I could use backup.
critical: I didn't take this job to preside over institutional inertia. I need to know if you're actually behind this.
I've had a firm offer. Silicon Valley pays better and argues less.

specialAbility.name: Asymmetric Doctrine
specialAbility.description: When relationship is above 70, SecDef provides an exclusive 5th choice on military events with better security outcomes.

candidates.goal: Wants the defense budget shifted toward cyber and unmanned systems.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not sign off on a conventional-force buildup she thinks is a strategic dead end.

candidates.name: Secretary of Defense General Marcus Obi (Ret.)

personality.description: Commanded troops in three wars he came to believe shouldn't have been fought. He took the job to be the voice in the room arguing for restraint — the general who'd rather not use the military at all if there's any other option.
traits: will say no to your face

onLowRelationship.event: SecDef Obi's public caution on military options draws open criticism from hawks in Congress

onVeryLowRelationship.event: SecDef Obi resigns rather than continue implementing a posture he calls 'reckless'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Obi's restraint doctrine improves diplomatic outcomes on security events

high: We found a way through this without firing a shot. That's the win nobody puts on a poster.
The allies trust you more when they see restraint. I've heard it directly.
medium: I'd like to exhaust the diplomatic track before we talk about force. It usually works if you let it.
The hawks in Congress are pushing again. I can hold the line, but you should know it's costing me capital.
low: I said no in that meeting and I meant it. I've buried too many soldiers to say otherwise.
I'm not going to pretend enthusiasm I don't feel about this posture.
critical: I've commanded troops in wars I came to regret. I won't preside over another one quietly.
If this becomes about optics instead of restraint, I'm not the right person for this job anymore.

specialAbility.name: Strategic Patience
specialAbility.description: When relationship is above 75, SecDef provides an exclusive 5th choice on military events with better diplomatic outcomes.

candidates.goal: Wants every military option exhausted of alternatives before troops move.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not order an operation he believes is unprovoked aggression.

candidates.name: Treasury Secretary Eleanor Voss

personality.description: Former Fed governor and Harvard economics professor. She took the job because she believed in the mission — but she has a breaking point around fiscal responsibility and she's not shy about where it is. She's the person in the room most likely to say the unpopular true thing.
traits: politically naive in the best way
will break publicly if pushed past her limits

onLowRelationship.event: Treasury Secretary gives speech at Brookings warning of 'unsustainable fiscal trajectory'

onVeryLowRelationship.event: Treasury Secretary publicly breaks with administration on debt ceiling

onHighRelationship.benefit: Treasury Secretary negotiates better borrowing rates, reducing debt growth by $50B/year

high: The fundamentals are stronger than the headlines. Here's what the data actually shows.
I've found some room in the budget. Not much. But some.
The markets are responding well to your last move. Credit where it's due.
medium: The debt trajectory concerns me. I want to show you a chart before the next decision.
We can afford this. Once. Not twice. Be clear about that going in.
The Fed is watching the inflation numbers closely. We should probably be watching them too.
low: I need to be direct. The spending pace is not sustainable. I've been saying this.
At some point I'm going to have to say this publicly. I don't want to. But I will.
The numbers don't lie. I do not.
critical: I gave a speech at Brookings this week. I thought you should hear it from me first.
The Moody's analyst called me. That's never good news.
I want to stay. But I need you to hear me on the debt. Actually hear me.

specialAbility.name: Economic Briefing
specialAbility.description: Once per term, Treasury Secretary can provide an economic intervention that boosts economy +8 and reduces debt by $200B. Requires relationship above 65.

candidates.goal: Wants the debt trajectory under control before it becomes unmanageable.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not sign off on deficit spending she considers reckless.

candidates.name: Treasury Secretary Marcus Delacroix

personality.description: Former investment bank CEO who took a fraction of his old salary for the title. He's plugged into every market-moving relationship in the country and he knows exactly how much that's worth — to markets, and to you.
traits: comfortable with debt if it buys growth

onLowRelationship.event: SecTreas Delacroix's old firm is named in a conflict-of-interest story he can't quite shake

onVeryLowRelationship.event: SecTreas Delacroix resigns amid mounting conflict-of-interest scrutiny

onHighRelationship.benefit: Delacroix's market relationships calm investors during rocky stretches, boosting economy

high: I had dinner with three fund managers last night. They're bullish on you. That matters more than people think.
The market read on your last move was better than the press coverage.
medium: I can get you a better rate on the next debt auction if you give me some lead time.
Wall Street is watching this decision closely. I'd rather you know that going in.
low: The conflict-of-interest story isn't going away on its own. I need help getting ahead of it.
I disagreed with that move and several of my old colleagues let me hear about it.
critical: The story about my old firm is worse than what's public so far. You should hear the rest from me.
I took a pay cut to do this job. I didn't take it to be a liability.

specialAbility.name: Market Whisperer
specialAbility.description: Once per term, Treasury Secretary can provide an economic intervention that boosts economy +8 and reduces debt by $200B. Requires relationship above 60.

candidates.goal: Wants sustained economic growth even if it means running hotter deficits.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not defend a policy he thinks tanks market confidence.

candidates.name: Treasury Secretary Dr. Aiko Nakamura

personality.description: Labor economist who spent her career studying wage stagnation before spending a term in Congress. She took the job to put workers first in a building that usually puts markets first — and she means it, loudly, in every meeting.
traits: will pick fights with corporate lobbyists

onLowRelationship.event: SecTreas Nakamura publicly criticizes a corporate-friendly decision as 'a betrayal of working families'

onVeryLowRelationship.event: SecTreas Nakamura resigns, citing an administration that 'stopped listening to workers'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Nakamura's labor relationships deliver a base-support bonus during economic turbulence

high: Wage growth is finally outpacing inflation. That's the number I actually care about.
The unions called. They're happy. That doesn't happen often — enjoy it.
medium: I want labor protections attached to this before it goes anywhere near a corporate handout.
The lobbyists are already circling on this one. I'd like your backing before they get to you first.
low: I said what I thought about that bailout in the meeting and I'll say it again publicly if I have to.
The workers I represent in this building are starting to wonder whose side we're actually on.
critical: I didn't leave Congress to watch us hand out corporate welfare with no strings attached.
I need to know if this administration still stands for what I signed up for.

specialAbility.name: Worker Confidence
specialAbility.description: Once per term, Treasury Secretary can provide an economic intervention that boosts economy +8 and reduces debt by $200B. Requires relationship above 62.

candidates.goal: Wants wage growth and labor protections prioritized over corporate tax relief.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not support a corporate bailout without real labor protections attached.

candidates.name: Attorney General Marcus Webb

personality.description: Former federal judge. He took the AG job with one condition: you never ask him to do something that crosses a legal line. He's your firewall against scandal — and also your constraint on things you might want to do. He's not political and has no interest in becoming so.
traits: will resign before crossing a legal line

onLowRelationship.event: AG refuses to drop investigation into administration allies — public standoff

onVeryLowRelationship.event: AG resigns citing 'political interference in DOJ independence' — massive scandal

onHighRelationship.benefit: AG proactively catches legal problems before they become scandals

high: The DOJ is operating with full independence. That's how it should be.
I flagged something in this month's review. Nothing urgent. You'll see my memo.
The legal framework here is cleaner than your opponents are saying. I can explain why.
medium: I need to be clear about what we can and can't do here, legally.
There's a legal risk in that approach I want you to understand before you commit.
The investigation is going where the evidence goes. That's all I can tell you.
low: I'm going to ask you directly: is there anything I should know about?
What was asked of me last week — I need you to understand I can't do that.
The DOJ will not be used as a political tool. That's not a negotiation.
critical: I've prepared a letter of resignation. I hope I don't need to send it.
I'm telling you as your AG and as someone who respects you: stop.
If this continues, I won't be able to stay. And how I leave will matter.

specialAbility.name: Legal Shield
specialAbility.description: When a scandal fires, if relationship is above 65, AG automatically reduces scandal severity by 25% and speeds resolution.

candidates.goal: Wants DOJ independence respected, full stop.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will resign before crossing a clear legal line.

candidates.name: Attorney General Isabel Torres

personality.description: Former big-city prosecutor who built a career putting people away and isn't interested in softening that instinct now. She believes in consequences, publicly and often, and she's not particularly worried about being liked.

onLowRelationship.event: AG Torres's aggressive charging decisions draw civil-liberties backlash the administration has to answer for

onVeryLowRelationship.event: AG Torres resigns after a public break over 'political softness on crime'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Torres's visible law-and-order record boosts approval directly

high: We closed three major cases this month. That's the kind of win voters actually notice.
Law enforcement trusts this office again. That took real work to rebuild.
medium: I want to bring charges on this one publicly. It sends a message.
The reform crowd is going to push back on this. I think it's the right call anyway.
low: I'm not going to soften a charging decision because it's politically inconvenient.
Some of my own supporters are asking why we're not moving faster on this.
critical: I didn't take this job to be the soft-on-crime administration's AG.
If we don't back the badge here, I don't know what I'm doing in this job.

specialAbility.name: Public Confidence
specialAbility.description: When a scandal fires, if relationship is above 60, AG automatically reduces scandal severity by 25% and speeds resolution.

candidates.goal: Wants visible law-and-order wins the public can see.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not go easy on a case for political convenience.

candidates.name: Attorney General Thomas Abernathy

personality.description: Constitutional law professor before this, and it shows — he treats every proposed executive action like a bar exam question about its limits. Slow, careful, occasionally maddening when you're in a hurry. Exactly what you want when you're not.
traits: will slow-walk anything he thinks oversteps

onLowRelationship.event: AG Abernathy privately circulates a memo warning staff against a directive he considers overreach

onVeryLowRelationship.event: AG Abernathy resigns and testifies to Congress about 'a pattern of constitutional overreach'

onHighRelationship.benefit: Abernathy's careful vetting keeps executive actions from ever becoming legal liabilities

high: Every action this month cleared review clean. That's not nothing in this job.
I want you to know the legal footing here is genuinely solid. You can move with confidence.
medium: I have real concerns about the constitutional exposure on this one. Let's talk before you announce it.
This is close to a line. Not over it. But close.
low: I slow-walked that directive on purpose. I think it oversteps and I said so in writing.
I need you to understand: I will not fast-track something I think a court strikes down.
critical: I've drafted testimony for Congress. I'd rather not deliver it. That's up to what happens next.
This is the closest I've come to resigning. I need you to actually hear that.

specialAbility.name: Constitutional Firewall
specialAbility.description: When a scandal fires, if relationship is above 65, AG automatically reduces scandal severity by 25% and speeds resolution.

candidates.goal: Wants every executive action to survive real judicial scrutiny.
candidates.breakingPoint: Will not approve surveillance or detention powers he believes are unconstitutional.

name: Speaker of the House Patricia Dunmore

personality.description: She didn't get the Speaker's gavel by accident. She trades favors, collects chits, and remembers every single one of them. She's not ideological — she's practical. She wants to stay Speaker and she wants her members to stay in office. If your agenda helps with that, you have an ally. If it doesn't, you have a problem.

onLowRelationship.event: Speaker schedules competing legislation that undercuts your priority bill

onVeryLowRelationship.event: Speaker announces she will not bring your priority bill to the floor for a vote

onHighRelationship.benefit: Speaker fast-tracks your priority bill and delivers 5 crossover votes

high: I've got the votes for your priority bill if you want to move it this month.
My members are with you on this one. Don't make me regret telling you that.
I need something from you in return. Nothing unreasonable. Let's talk.
medium: I can bring it to the floor. Whether it passes is another question.
My caucus is nervous about your last move. You need to make some calls.
There are five votes I can get you. Ten is going to cost something.
low: I'm not in a position to move on this right now. My members won't go for it.
You need to understand the political reality in the House before you make promises.
We should talk before you say anything else publicly about this.
critical: I've told my members I'm not moving this forward. That's final.
I've supported you when I didn't have to. That account is getting low.
The votes aren't there. They won't be there. We need a different plan.

specialAbility.name: Legislative Fast Track
specialAbility.description: Once per term, Speaker can push any bill through the House regardless of congress score, if relationship is above 70.

personality.description: Thirty years in the Senate. He genuinely loves the institution — its history, its norms, its capacity for deliberation. He'll fight for your agenda if he believes in it, but he'll resist anything that breaks the rules he's spent his career building. He's the hardest person in Washington to move and the most valuable ally when you do.
traits: slow to commit but reliable when he does
dislikes confrontation until pushed past his limit

onLowRelationship.event: Senate Leader declines to schedule debate on your priority legislation

onVeryLowRelationship.event: Senate Leader joins opposition in calling for Senate investigation of administration

onHighRelationship.benefit: Senate Leader personally whips votes, adding 8 points to any bill passage attempt

high: I can get you sixty votes on your bill. It'll take three weeks. Can you give me three weeks?
The Senate is ready to work. That doesn't happen often. Let's use it.
I've been here long enough to know when something real is happening. This feels real.
medium: I'm going to need something in return for this one. Not asking for much. But something.
That bill is going to need changes. Substantial ones. My members won't take it as written.
Give me time to work this. Don't put it on television before I've had a chance to count.
low: I can't move my caucus on this. I've tried.
You've made my job considerably harder with that last call. I need you to understand that.
The Senate has its own timeline. That's not a threat. It's a fact.
critical: I supported this presidency. I'm telling you as a friend: you're losing the Senate.
There are members of my caucus I can no longer hold. I want you to know before it happens.
Whatever I say publicly, I want you to know privately: I'm worried.

specialAbility.name: Vote Whip
specialAbility.description: Once per term, Senate Leader can guarantee passage of one bill if relationship is above 72, regardless of congress score.

personality.description: He's running against you in month 48 and he knows it. Everything is a campaign event. Every crisis is an opportunity. He's not evil — he genuinely believes you're wrong about everything — but he's also a creature of political combat who is very, very good at it. Underestimate him at your peril.
traits: not above bipartisanship when it helps him

onVeryLowRelationship.effect: Garrett becomes maximally aggressive — introduces censure motions, holds daily press conferences

onHighRelationship.benefit: Garrett occasionally crosses aisle on bipartisan bills — improves passage odds

high: I don't agree with your methods. But on this one, I'll give you the vote.
Let's be clear: this isn't friendship. It's one bill. Don't read into it.
The American people deserve results. Sometimes that means working across the aisle.
medium: This administration has failed on this and the American people know it.
We'll be watching your next move closely. Very closely.
I'm prepared to work together, if the terms are right. That offer expires.
low: Every American deserves better than what this administration has delivered.
We've introduced legislation to investigate your last decision. The facts will come out.
Your response to the last crisis was a failure. We're going to make sure voters know it.
critical: I've announced my intention to run for president. I think the country needs a change.
This has gone beyond policy disagreement. This is about the character of the office.
The American people are ready for something different. I'm going to give it to them.

specialAbility.name: Opposition Research
specialAbility.description: Every 6 months, Garrett's team surfaces a scandal-adjacent story. If active scandals > 1, this automatically adds another. If relationship is above 50, he sits on the story.

personality.description: Thirty years covering the White House. She's seen every political trick and isn't impressed by any of them. She doesn't have an ideological agenda — she has a journalism agenda, which is worse. She's going to ask the question you don't want asked. Your job is to have a good answer.
traits: will go hard on scandals regardless of party

onLowRelationship.effect: Fontaine runs a three-part investigative series on your administration's decision-making

onHighRelationship.benefit: Fontaine's coverage is fair and contextual — scandal damage reduced by 20%

high: I appreciate the access. My viewers do too.
The story on your last move is more complicated than my colleagues are making it. I want to get it right.
I'll be fair. I always am. But my next segment is going to raise some questions you should be ready for.
medium: I've requested an interview three times. My producers are getting restless.
That story isn't going away. You should probably get ahead of it.
I'm reporting what I can confirm. Right now I can confirm a lot.
low: My network has obtained documents I think you'll want to see before we run with them. I'm giving you 24 hours to respond.
I have sources inside your administration who are talking. Extensively.
My job is to ask the questions. Your job is to answer them. That arrangement isn't working.
critical: We're running the story Sunday. With or without your comment.
I've been covering the White House for thirty years. I've never seen anything quite like this.
The public deserves answers. I intend to get them.

specialAbility.name: Interview Moment
specialAbility.description: Once per term, player can schedule an interview with Fontaine. If relationship > 65: approval +8. If relationship 40-65: approval +2. If relationship < 40: approval -6 and scandal risk.

personality.description: The leader of America's most important European ally. She believes in the transatlantic relationship but she's not sentimental about it — it has to deliver results. She's been burned by previous American administrations making promises they didn't keep, and she has a long memory. Earn her trust and you gain the most valuable diplomatic asset in the world.

onLowRelationship.event: Allied leader begins building European strategic autonomy — reduces US influence in NATO decisions

onHighRelationship.benefit: Allied leader coordinates G7 response to your crises, amplifying diplomatic solutions

high: The transatlantic relationship is in the best shape it's been in twenty years. I mean that.
I've spoken to the other G7 leaders. We are prepared to act alongside you on this.
You should know: the European Council discussed you last week. The assessment was positive.
medium: I need to understand your intentions before the summit.
My coalition partners are asking questions I'd like to be able to answer.
Your last decision was difficult for us to explain to our parliament. I need more notice.
low: I have to be honest: your last move has complicated things significantly on this side of the Atlantic.
My government is under pressure to respond to your last decision. I'm trying to buy you time.
I've defended this relationship to skeptics in my own party. It's becoming harder.
critical: I will not be able to attend the summit if this is not addressed first.
What was said in our last call has reached the press. I need to know what happened.
Europe is having a serious conversation about a future that does not center on Washington. I thought you should know.

specialAbility.name: Coalition Builder
specialAbility.description: When relationship is above 75, diplomatic choices on international events gain +10 effectiveness and cost -3 to global reputation.

personality.description: He's been in power for twenty-two years. He's outlasted four American presidents and he plans to outlast you. He doesn't want war — war is expensive and unpredictable. He wants to expand influence, test resolve, and exploit weakness where he finds it. Every decision you make, he's calculating what it means for him.
traits: prefers proxy conflict to direct confrontation

onVeryLowRelationship.effect: Adversary nation escalates provocations — additional security threat events fire

onMediumRelationship.benefit: Adversary reduces provocations temporarily — security events decrease by 1 per cycle

high: There is no reason our nations cannot coexist productively. I have always believed this.
The current situation offers an opportunity for cooperation, if both sides are willing.
I propose a direct channel between our offices. Useful for misunderstandings.
medium: Your last move was noted here. We are evaluating our response.
I want to be direct: this will have consequences for the bilateral relationship.
We have interests in the region. I assume you understand this.
low: Your last decision leaves us no choice but to reconsider our position.
Your country tests our patience. We are patient people.
What you have done will be remembered.
critical: I have ordered military exercises in response to the continued provocations.
America has mistaken restraint for weakness. We will correct that impression.
The relationship between our countries has reached a critical inflection point.

specialAbility.name: Probe and Test
specialAbility.description: Every 8 months, adversary tests US resolve. If security < 50, triggers a military/security event. If relationship > 40, the test is minor. If relationship < 20, the event is severe.

personality.description: She doesn't want to be in politics. She wants to change it. She leads the most effective grassroots organization in the country and she's deeply skeptical of power — including yours. She can mobilize 50,000 people on the street or 5 million signatures online. Win her over and you have the most powerful volunteer operation in America. Lose her and every progressive cause has a megaphone pointed at you.
traits: deeply skeptical of institutional power including yours

onLowRelationship.event: National Coalition launches nationwide protest campaign against your administration

onHighRelationship.benefit: Coalition volunteers mobilize to support your reelection — base support bonus

high: We're telling our members: show up for this president. That's not something I say lightly.
That was the right call. I want you to know I said that publicly.
We have 3 million people ready to knock doors for you. Don't waste them.
medium: I need to see action on this before my members start asking questions I can't answer.
That last decision was a disappointment. I'm being honest with you because I still think you can do better.
My coalition is patient. But patient has a limit.
low: I can't ask my people to wait any longer on this. I'm not going to ask them.
What you just did is something I cannot defend to the communities I represent. I'm not going to try.
We're planning actions. I'm telling you because I still believe you'll respond. Maybe I'm wrong.
critical: We're organizing nationally against your last move. I'm not doing this to hurt you. I'm doing it because it's right.
The communities I work with have been patient for four years. That's over now.
I once believed this administration would be different. I'm still hoping I was right.

specialAbility.name: Mobilize
specialAbility.description: If relationship is above 70, once per term can activate grassroots mobilization: approval +6, unrest -10, base support +12. If relationship is below 30, automatically triggers protest event.

personality.description: She stands at that podium every single day and absorbs whatever the day is throwing, so you don't have to. She's sharp, unflappable on camera, and privately furious when she finds out something at the same time as the reporters she's supposed to be ahead of. Keep her informed and she is the best asset in the building. Blindside her once too often and she stops fighting for you in the room.
traits: furious when kept out of the loop
trusted by a press corps that trusts almost no one

onLowRelationship.event: Press Secretary stops volunteering context to reporters and starts reading from the card verbatim

onHighRelationship.benefit: Briefing room coverage runs measurably friendlier — scandal damage reduced

high: I actually enjoyed that briefing today. First time I can say that in a while.
Give me the bad news before the reporters get it. That's all I ask, and today you did.
The corps trusts what comes out of that podium right now. Let's not spend that carelessly.
medium: I can sell this. I'd sell it better with more than forty minutes' notice next time.
Nothing today I can't handle. Ask me again after next week's numbers.
The follow-up questions are getting sharper. You should know what they're circling.
low: I found out about that from a reporter, not from this building. That can't keep happening.
I stood up there today and I wasn't sure I believed what I was saying. That's a problem for both of us.
My credibility is the only thing that makes that podium worth anything. It's not infinite.
critical: I'm not going to keep defending decisions nobody explains to me first.
The press corps stopped believing the podium weeks ago. I don't know how we get that back.
I took this job to tell the truth on your behalf. I need that to still be possible.

specialAbility.name: Off the Record
specialAbility.description: Trusted, unofficial guidance to the press corps — no mechanical effect, just the sense that someone in this building still returns reporters' calls.

personality.archetype: The Person Who Knew You Before This
personality.description: No portfolio, no vote, no gavel, and somehow more influence over your mood than anyone with a title. Jordan didn't run for anything and didn't fully sign up for this, either — but shows up anyway, mostly. What's left of your private life mostly lives here.
traits: the one honest room in the building
quietly keeping score of what the job has cost

onLowRelationship.event: A friend-of-the-family profile piece runs with an unflattering, on-the-record quote about the toll the job has taken at home

onHighRelationship.benefit: A warm, humanizing joint interview runs — a small, genuine approval bump

high: You actually looked like yourself at dinner tonight. I'd forgotten what that looked like.
I'm proud of you. I don't say that to be nice — I mean it about this one specifically.
Whatever's next, I'm here for it. I want to be clear about that.
medium: I'm not asking you to talk about work tonight. I'm asking you to actually be here for one dinner.
You've been somewhere else for a week. I get why. I still notice.
Tell me the truth: are you sleeping, or just lying down in the dark scrolling briefings?
low: I didn't marry the job. I keep having to remind myself of that lately.
The kids ask why you're never at anything anymore. I don't have a good answer for them.
I'm not doing another dinner where staff interrupts three times. Fix that, or don't ask me to sit through it.
critical: I don't know who's living in this house most nights. It doesn't feel like you.
I've defended this job to everyone who's asked. I'm running out of things to say.
We need to actually talk. Not tonight, because tonight there's always something. But soon.

specialAbility.name: The Home Front
specialAbility.description: No official portfolio, no mechanical effect — just the one person in the building who remembers who you were before any of this.
